assessment.signage: Tattoo Shop; Modern Lovers Ink; Primitive Ink tattoos

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade is occupied and readable from the street, with contemporary storefront alterations layered onto the historic building form.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple fascia and coping with limited historic trim visible.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney clearly visible from the selected frontage view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Segmental arched brick voussoirs over second-floor windows. Original brick facade legible above commercial ground floor.
photo_observations.storefront_description: Currently closed or papered-over storefront with awning and heavy graffiti.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Ground floor completely converted to commercial storefronts. Upper facade retains original red brick with arched window heads. Some graffiti on brick.
photo_observations.notes: HCD typology is House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable. The original house-form massing is clearly legible above the commercial ground floor. Segmental arched window heads confirm Victorian-era construction.
photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: red brick upper facade with graffiti; commercial storefronts at ground level with pink/black signage and wood-panelled display area
photo_observations.window_details: Two upper-floor windows with segmental arched brick heads visible in red brick wall. Ground floor fully commercial.
photo_observations.door_details: Single side commercial door beside the shuttered frontage.
photo_observations.signage_observed: For lease sign in the front window.

facade_description: 283 Augusta Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade is organized as a storefront base with 2 upper-window bays, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the ear
